abstract: The distribution of ions at the air/water interface is a fundamental driver of numerous chemical and environmental processes. For years, the prevailing scientific consensus has suggested that large, polarizable ions are surface-active, congregating at the very top of the liquid phase to generate electric fields that dictate interfacial water organization. In this study, we challenge this conventional model using a combination of heterodyne-detected vibrational sum-frequency generation and neural network-assisted ab initio molecular dynamics simulations. Our findings reveal that ions in common electrolyte solutions do not reside at the immediate surface, but are instead situated in a distinct subsurface region. This creates a stratified interface characterized by a pristine, ion-depleted outermost layer and an ion-enriched layer underneath. This subsurface stratification provides a new conceptual framework for understanding how ions reorganize water molecules at the boundary between air and water.
